title: Cottage pie
subtitle: Beef mince under buttered mash.
excerpt: The beef sibling of shepherd's pie. Slow-cooked minced beef in a deep gravy of onions, carrots, and a slug of red wine, topped with buttered mash and baked until the peaks are forked and gold-brown. Four with seconds.
sourceNotes: The shepherd's / cottage pie split (lamb / beef) settled in late-Victorian English household manuals. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), describes 'baked minced beef under potato' as a standby of the small household. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), public domain, on flouring the mince to thicken its own gravy.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes raw or cooked. Raw: assemble in a foil tray, freeze up to three months, bake from cold at 180°C for 50 minutes. Cooked: portion, cool, wrap; reheat under a covered foil at 170°C for 30 minutes, then grill.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the beef base; it freezes flat. Top with fresh mash on the day; mash frozen on the pie weeps and discolours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to a day ahead and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

content.text: Beef mince browned hard and built into a slow gravy of onion, carrot, celery, tomato purée, red wine, and beef stock. A thick mash topping, beaten with butter and milk, forked into peaks that crisp gold-brown in a hot oven. Forty minutes of cooking, twenty-five of baking, ten of resting. The cottage pie is the beef sibling of shepherd's pie; the names settled in the late Victorian era when British households named their pies for the animal that gave the meat.

Beef mince behaves differently from lamb. It has less fat per gram and a leaner flavour, so the gravy needs more help: a splash of red wine, a teaspoon of anchovy paste, a hard sear on the meat to build fond on the pan base. The mash topping carries the same rules either way.

items.prepNote: invisible on the tongue, gives backbone
Maris Piper or King Edward, peeled and quartered

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide sauté pan over a hot hob until it shimmers. Crumble {{beef-mince}} of beef mince into the pan in walnut-sized clumps. Don't stir for three minutes; let the underside catch and brown deep. Turn the clumps and brown the second side. Lift the mince onto a plate and leave the fond behind.

Drop the heat. Add {{onion}} diced onion, {{carrot}} diced carrots, and {{celery}} diced celery to the same pan with the rendered beef fat. Cook gently for 10 minutes, stirring now and then. Stir in {{garlic}} of chopped garlic, the {{thyme}} of thyme leaves, and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Cook two more minutes.

Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and {{anchovy-paste}} of anchovy paste. Cook two minutes until the purée darkens to brick. Scatter {{plain-flour}} of flour over the vegetables; stir. Return the beef and any resting juices. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and let it bubble hard for two minutes — the alcohol burns off. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire and {{stock-beef}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer.
Cook gently, partly covered, on the lowest setting for 30 minutes. The liquid reduces by a third; the gravy coats the back of a spoon. Fish out the bay leaves. Taste and adjust salt and pepper.

Cover {{potato}} of peeled, quartered potatoes with cold salted water. Bring to the boil. Simmer 18 minutes until a knife slides in cleanly. Drain. Return to the dry hot pan for one minute to steam-dry. Mash. Beat in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, then {{whole-milk}} of warmed milk. Season generously.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Tip the mince into a 24 × 30 cm baking tin. Spoon the mash on in dollops; spread to the edges so the gravy is sealed in. Drag a fork through the top to draw peaks; scatter {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar if using. Bake 25 minutes until golden, then a minute or two under a hot grill to crisp the peaks. Rest 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Beef mince was steamed not seared, or the gravy wasn't reduced long enough.
items.fix: Brown in walnut clumps in a single layer; simmer 30 minutes until the gravy clings to a spoon.

items.cause: Mash too wet, or the meat layer too liquid at assembly.
items.fix: Steam-dry the potatoes for a minute before fat goes in. Cool the mince base for 10 minutes before topping.

items.cause: Cold from the fridge and baked at full heat.
items.fix: Bring to room temperature first, or bake at 180°C for 40 minutes from cold, then grill briefly.
Salt after browning; the anchovy paste is invisible on the tongue but lifts the meat.

items.cause: Cheese added too early or grilled too long.
items.fix: Scatter cheese before grilling; one to two minutes under the grill, no more.

content.text: Swap beef for lamb mince and you have shepherd's pie. A handful of frozen peas stirred through the mince in the last five minutes adds colour. Sweet potato mash gives a softer, sweeter top; half-and-half with regular potato is a balanced halfway version. A vegetarian cottage pie works with 400 g of cooked Puy lentils in place of the beef and vegetable stock instead of beef.

Cottage pie sits at the centre of the British weeknight repertoire. It uses Monday's leftover mince or Sunday's chopped roast, builds slowly on a winter evening, and tastes better the next day. Eat it with buttered cabbage or a heap of peas, a spoon of pickled red cabbage if there's a jar in the fridge, and a glass of the wine the gravy was built from.
